title: Scope and coverage
text: EU261 generally applies to flights departing from EU/EEA/CH airports, and to flights arriving in the EU operated by an EU/EEA/CH carrier.

title: Delay threshold
text: Court of Justice case law treats arrival delay of 3 hours or more similarly to cancellation for compensation purposes, unless extraordinary circumstances apply.

title: Compensation bands
text: Typical compensation amounts are EUR 250 for flights up to 1500 km, EUR 400 for intra-EU flights over 1500 km and many other flights between 1500 and 3500 km, and EUR 600 for longer flights.

title: Arrival delay basis
text: Delay duration for compensation is based on arrival at final destination, not departure delay.

title: Extraordinary circumstances
text: Airlines may reject compensation if they prove extraordinary circumstances that could not have been avoided even with all reasonable measures.

title: Right to care
text: For long delays, passengers are entitled to care such as meals, refreshments, communications, and in some cases accommodation and transport.

title: Re-routing and refunds
text: In major disruptions, passengers can choose reimbursement or re-routing under comparable transport conditions.

title: Evidence checklist
text: Claimants should keep boarding pass, booking confirmation, delay notices, receipts, and any communication with the airline.

title: Claim data needed
text: Airlines usually require passenger identity, flight number, travel date, route, and description of disruption.

title: Compensation currency
text: Compensation amounts under EU261 are commonly denominated in euros.

title: Not legal advice
text: This assistant provides general guidance and templates, not legal advice; final eligibility can depend on detailed facts and local court interpretation.

title: Connecting flights
text: For connecting journeys under one booking, delay at final destination is relevant to compensation analysis.

title: Burden of proof
text: Airline generally bears burden to prove extraordinary circumstances when denying compensation.

title: Operational issues
text: Routine technical or operational problems are often not considered extraordinary circumstances.

title: Minimum intake
text: A minimal claim intake should include provider, flight number, flight date, route, delay at arrival, passenger name and contact email.
